questions.question: Explain the difference between dense index and sparse index with appropriate examples.
keyPoints: Dense index has entry for every record; sparse index has entries for some records
Dense index provides faster lookup; sparse index uses less storage
Example: Sparse index typically stores first record of each data block
Dense index suitable for small tables; sparse index for large tables with sequential access

questions.question: What are the advantages and disadvantages of indexing in DBMS?
keyPoints: Advantages: Faster data retrieval, reduced I/O operations, efficient WHERE clause processing
Disadvantages: Slower write operations, increased storage space, index maintenance overhead
Trade-off between read performance and write performance

questions.question: Explain B+ tree index structure and why it is preferred over B-tree for database indexing.
keyPoints: B+ tree has all data pointers at leaf nodes only
Leaf nodes are linked for efficient range queries
Internal nodes store only keys, allowing more fan-out
More predictable performance as all accesses go to same depth
Better for disk-based storage due to higher fan-out

questions.question: Describe the concept of multi-level indexing and its benefits.
keyPoints: Multi-level indexing creates hierarchy of indexes (2-3 levels)
First level is dense index pointing to data
Higher levels are sparse indexes pointing to lower levels
Reduces I/O operations by narrowing search at each level
Minimizes disk accesses needed to reach data records

questions.question: What is a clustering index? How does it differ from a non-clustering index?
keyPoints: Clustering index organizes actual data records based on indexed column values
Only one clustering index per table as data is physically ordered
Non-clustering index creates separate structure with pointers to data
Multiple non-clustering indexes possible per table
Non-clustering requires additional lookup after finding index entry

questions.question: Explain the concept of index selectivity. Why is it important for query optimization?
keyPoints: Index selectivity = (Number of distinct values / Total records)
High selectivity (close to 1) means more unique values - index is efficient
Low selectivity means many duplicate values - index provides little benefit
Optimizer chooses to use index only if selectivity is above threshold
Low selectivity indexes may lead to full table scan instead

questions.question: Discuss the factors that should be considered before creating an index on a database table.
keyPoints: Column usage in WHERE clauses and JOIN conditions
Column cardinality (number of unique values)
Table size and query frequency
Write vs Read ratio of the table
Storage overhead and maintenance cost

questions.question: Compare and contrast primary index, secondary index, clustering index, and non-clustering index with examples.
keyPoints: Primary index: created on primary key, dense, one per table, determines physical order
Clustering index: created on non-key attributes, defines physical grouping of records
Secondary index: created on any other column, provides alternative access paths, can be multiple
Non-clustering index: same as secondary, separate from physical data storage
Key differences: number per table, effect on physical storage, density (dense/sparse)
Examples: Employee table - primary on EMP_ID, clustering on DEPT_NO, secondary on EMAIL

questions.question: Explain the structure and operations of B+ tree with a diagram. How does it support efficient search, insertion, and deletion operations?
keyPoints: Structure: Root, internal nodes, leaf nodes all at same depth
Internal nodes contain key values and child pointers only
Leaf nodes contain data pointers and are linked horizontally
Search: Traverse from root to leaf using key comparisons
Insert: Find leaf, insert key; split if node overflows, propagate up
Delete: Remove key from leaf; merge or borrow if underflow
All leaf nodes at same level ensures O(log n) for all operations
Linked leaves enable efficient range queries

questions.question: Describe the process of query execution using an index. What are the steps involved when the database optimizer decides to use an index?
keyPoints: Optimizer analyzes query and checks WHERE clause conditions
Evaluates index selectivity and cost of different access paths
Chooses between index scan and full table scan based on cost
If index chosen: traverse index tree to find matching entries
Retrieve data records using pointers from index entries
For non-clustering index: additional I/O for each record ( Bookmark lookup )
For clustering index: records already ordered, fewer I/O needed
Cost factors: index height, number of matching records, I/O cost per page

questions.question: Explain composite indexing in databases. How does column order affect the usage of composite indexes? Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Composite index created on two or more columns
Index entries sorted first by first column, then second, etc.
Column order matters for query optimization
Query can use index if it includes leading columns in WHERE clause
Example: Index on (DEPT, SALARY) can be used for WHERE DEPT='IT' or WHERE DEPT='IT' AND SALARY>50000
Cannot efficiently use for WHERE SALARY>50000 alone
Explain concept of equality and range queries on composite indexes
Discuss index covering and include columns for optimization
